title: Valve Authority Range for HVAC Coil Applications
summary: Valve authorities in the range of 30 to 50% are adequate for HVAC coil applications, with higher Δt coils requiring less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided.
evidence_quote: authorities in the range of 30 to 50% (for piping systems with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios) are adequate for HVAC coil applications, with higher Δt coils requiring less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided

title: Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Water Temperature Range
summary: Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.
evidence_quote: They are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.

title: Historical Hot-Water Heating System Design Parameters
summary: In the United States, hot-water heating systems were historically designed for a 93°C supply water temperature and an 11 K temperature drop.
evidence_quote: In the United States, hot-water heating systems were historically designed for a 93°C supply water temperature and an 11 K temperature drop.

title: Coanda Effect Description
summary: The Coanda effect causes a jet moving adjacent to a surface to remain in contact with the surface throughout the length of throw, counteracting the drop of a cool airstream.

structured_payload.description: jet remains in contact with surface, counteracts drop of cool airstream

evidence_quote: a jet moving adjacent to or in contact with an impermeable wall or ceiling creates a low-pressure area immediately adjacent to that surface, causing the air to remain in contact with the surface substantially throughout the length of throw. This Coanda effect, also referred to as the surface or ceiling effect, counteracts, to varying extents, the negative-buoyancy-driven the drop of a horizontally projected cool airstream.

title: AHRI Standard 410 Coil Rating Parameters
summary: Coils are rated within parameters: entering air dry-bulb 18 to 38°C, wet-bulb 15 to 30°C, air face velocity 1 to 4 m/s, evaporator refrigerant saturation temperature –1 to 13°C, entering chilled-water temperature 1.5 to 18°C, water velocity 0.3 to 2.4 m/s.
evidence_quote: Entering air dry-bulb temperature: 18 to 38°C Entering air wet-bulb temperature: 15 to 30°C Air face velocity: 1 to 4 m/s Evaporator refrigerant saturation temperature: –1 to 13°C Entering chilled-water temperature: 1.5 to 18°C Water velocity: 0.3 to 2.4 m/s

title: Ethylene Glycol Solution Coil Rating Parameters
summary: For ethylene glycol solution: 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, –18 to 32°C entering dry-bulb, 15 to 27°C entering wet-bulb, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass.
evidence_quote: For ethylene glycol solution: 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, –18 to 32°C entering dry-bulb temperature, 15 to 27°C entering wet-bulb temperature, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass

title: Dehumidifying Coil Sensible Heat Ratio Range
summary: The air-side ratio of sensible to total heat removed by dehumidifying coils varies from about 0.6 to 1.0.
evidence_quote: The air-side ratio of sensible to total heat removed by dehumidifying coils varies in practice from about 0.6 to 1.0

title: Typical Air Face Velocity for Dehumidifying Coils
summary: Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.
evidence_quote: Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.

title: Standard Rating Point for Unitary Equipment
summary: A standard rating point has been 54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kilowatt for unitary equipment.

structured_payload.value: 54 L/s per kW, max 60 L/s per kW

evidence_quote: a standard rating point has been 54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kilowatt for unitary equipment.

title: Refrigerant Superheat for Unitary Equipment Balance
summary: Refrigerant duty would be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appropriate balance at 7°C saturated suction.
evidence_quote: Refrigerant (e.g., R-22) duty would be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appropriate balance at 7°C saturated suction.

title: Water Coil Circuitry Standard Conditions
summary: For water coils, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect).
evidence_quote: For water coils, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect).

title: Standard Entering Air Conditions for Comfort Ratings
summary: The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are representative of the entering air conditions encountered in many comfort operations.
evidence_quote: The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are representative of the entering air conditions encountered in many comfort operations.

title: Standard Air Density for Airflow Ratings
summary: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.
evidence_quote: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.

title: Freezing Condition for Dehumidification Coils
summary: Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.
evidence_quote: Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated

title: Airflow Resistance Reduction with Hydrophilic Coating
summary: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil.
evidence_quote: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil.
